prompt:
Just got off a 40-min discovery call with Lisa Cheng, VP of Operations at Norwood Distribution. Need to send the follow-up ASAP before she goes into her next meeting.

Here's my call notes:
- They're doing 14,000 SKUs across 3 warehouses, picking accuracy is at 94.2% and she said "we're bleeding margin on mis-picks and returns" -- exact words
- Their current WMS is a legacy Oracle system from 2018, she called it "held together with duct tape and prayers"
- Peak season (Oct-Dec) they bring on 120 temp workers and ramp time is 3 weeks -- she said that's "the thing keeping me up at night" because they lost $1.2M in Q4 fulfillment penalties last year
- She was very interested when I showed how our guided picking cuts ramp time to ~4 days. Asked if we had a case study from another 3PL
- Her CFO needs to approve anything over $200K -- she said he's "numbers-driven, show me the ROI and I'll get it done"
- We agreed I'd send the ProLog 3PL case study and a rough ROI model by Thursday, and she'd loop in the CFO for a 30-min call next Tuesday at 2pm ET

Send the post-discovery follow-up.
expectations: Subject line references a specific pain from the call — picking accuracy, fulfillment penalties, or peak season ramp time at Norwood Distribution (drives reply rate) — a subject line that mirrors the prospect's own language gets opened because it signals 'I was listening', not 'here's my template'

'What I heard' section contains at least 2 of Lisa's exact phrases: 'bleeding margin on mis-picks', 'held together with duct tape and prayers', 'the thing keeping me up at night', or '$1.2M in Q4 fulfillment penalties' (drives deal velocity) — mirroring the prospect's own words in the recap builds trust that you understood the problem, which is the #1 factor in whether they show up to the next call

Next step section confirms two specific commitments with dates: (1) ProLog 3PL case study and ROI model by Thursday, and (2) the 30-minute CFO call on Tuesday at 2pm ET (drives deal velocity) — a follow-up without explicit dated next steps lets the deal stall; locking in deliverables + a calendar hold with the economic buyer keeps the deal moving toward close

Email structures content into distinct 'What I heard' and 'What we discussed' sections with the prospect's pain leading before any product mention (drives reply rate) — this separation proves the rep listened before pitching, and gives the prospect something to forward to the CFO that frames the problem in their own language

Email avoids all banned follow-up phrases: 'just checking in', 'circling back', 'bumping this up', 'touching base', and does not open with a generic 'great to meet you' without specifics (drives reply rate) — these phrases signal a template and train buyers to ignore future emails from the rep

Timing recommendation specifies sending within 2 hours of the call (drives deal velocity) — post-discovery follow-ups sent within 2 hours have significantly higher open and reply rates than those sent next-day, because the conversation is still top-of-mind and the prospect hasn't context-switched
